coredata.dc:title: Solar-blind ultraviolet-visible dual-spectral photodetector based on carbon nanodots/flexible-silicon heterojunction
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Taihao
Fang, Yong
Zhu, Mengru
Zhao, Zhiwei
Lei, Wei
Zhu, Zhuoya
Jiang, Helong
coredata.dc:description: Flexible solar-blind ultraviolet (UV) photodetectors (PDs) have important applications in biomedical and wearable electronic devices. Herein, a dual-spectral carbon nanodots/flexible n-silicon heterojunction photodetector is reported that always shows a photo response in UV illumination, while in visible illumination there is a photo response only in forward bias. The working mechanism of the carbon nanodots/silicon heterojunction was investigated. At 5 V bias, the responsivity of the device was 30.5 mA/W and 93.7 mA/W under the 254 nm illumination and the visible illumination, respectively. And the detectivity was 1.61 × 1011 Jones and 2.14 × 1011 Jones, respectively. The photocurrent of the device can reach 91 % or more of the original under the 0–0.6 % bending strain.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar-blind ultraviolet
Photodetector
Dual spectra
Flexible
Carbon nanodots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of a dual-spectral CNDS/flexible silicon PD preparation process. SEM images of the silicon wafer etched at (b) 2 h 20 mins, (c) 2 h 30 mins, and (d) 2 h 33 mins. Bending experimental diagram of ultrathin silicon wafers with the thickness of (e) 37.5 µm, (f) 10.55 µm, and (g) 8.75 µm.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of (a) silicon wafers with different thicknesses, and (b) CNDs. Inset: Tauc plot of the direct energy. (c) I-V characteristics of the photodetectors in the dark and under the illumination of 254 nm and visible light. (d) I-V curves at different power densities of 254 nm. (e) photocurrent–power densities experiment and fit curves at 254 nm. (f) R-power curve at 254 nm. (g) I-V curves at different visible light powers densities. (h) photocurrent–power experiment and fit curves under visible light. (i) R-power curve under visible light. (j) The I-V characteristic of CNDs/ultrathin n-Si heterojunction photodetector under the chopped illumination of 254 nm (2.5 mW/cm2). (k) Rise time and decay time of a single on/off cycle when switched at 254 nm.

(a) I-t curves under different bending strains. (b) photo and dark current-Strain curves. (c) response time -Strain curves. (d) D* -Strain and R-Strain curves. (e) I-t curve of the detector after undergoing different cycles of bending at 0.2 % strain; (f) Curve of response time with the number of bending cycles.
I-T curves at one-month interval placement of the dual-spectral CNDS/flexible silicon heterojunction photodetector under 254 nm, 0.3 mW/cm2, at 3 V.

Energy band schematic of CNDs/silicon heterojunction under forward bias with (a) 254 nm irradiation, (b) visible irradiation, (c)responsivity of the device as a function of wavelength at 3 V; and under reserve bias with (c) 254 nm irradiation, (d) visible irradiation, and (f) responsivity of the device as a function of wavelength at − 3 V.
Performance Comparison of Typical Solar-blind Ultraviolet Photodetectors.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: #: A white light LED covering visible range.
*: A halogen lamp ranges from 330 nm to 1100 nm.

head.ce:title: Solar-blind ultraviolet-visible dual-spectral photodetector based on carbon nanodots/flexible-silicon heterojunction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Integrated Circuits, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Integrated Circuits, Southeast University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic Science and Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic Science and Engineering, Southeast University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronic Science and Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Lake Science and Environment, Nanjing Institute of Geography and Limnology Chinese Academy of Sciences, Nanjing 210008, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Lake Science and Environment, Nanjing Institute of Geography and Limnology Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Lake Science and Environment, Nanjing Institute of Geography and Limnology Chinese Academy of Sciences, Nanjing 210008, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flexible solar-blind ultraviolet (UV) photodetectors (PDs) have important applications in biomedical and wearable electronic devices. Herein, a dual-spectral carbon nanodots/flexible n-silicon heterojunction photodetector is reported that always shows a photo response in UV illumination, while in visible illumination there is a photo response only in forward bias. The working mechanism of the carbon nanodots/silicon heterojunction was investigated. At 5 V bias, the responsivity of the device was 30.5 mA/W and 93.7 mA/W under the 254 nm illumination and the visible illumination, respectively. And the detectivity was 1.61 × 1011 Jones and 2.14 × 1011 Jones, respectively. The photocurrent of the device can reach 91 % or more of the original under the 0–0.6 % bending strain.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar radiation in the specific wavelength range of 200–280 nm is hard to reach the earth's surface due to strong absorption by the ozone layer, so it is also called solar-blind ultraviolet (UV) radiation. Thus, this results in a low solar-blind UV interference on the earth's surface, making the solar-blind UV detector fulfill the needs for essential applications, including flame detection [1], remote control [2], missile warning [3], short-distance communications [4], and other areas of interest. To date, it shows promise for applications in the wearable photodetection field to manufacture a solar-blind UV–visible dual-spectral flexible photodetector (PD), that achieves accurate positioning through image fusion technology [5–7]. However, the realization of a dual-spectral flexible PD is still challenging due to two main technical obstacles. The first is the choice of a photosensitive semiconductor with tunable absorption and flexibility. Secondly, and more importantly, the implementation of a dual-spectral PD requires not only strategies to integrate multiple flexible materials with different band gaps into the same PD but also well-designed device architectures to access the two independent optical absorptions.

Currently, most solar-blind UV photodetectors are manufactured with rigid materials that are difficult to bend or fold. The device performance will be greatly reduced due to deformation, which limits its application in some new flexible devices. Compared to rigid substrate-based devices, flexible devices can match the shape requirements, achieve normal operation requirements under bending or tensile strain, and generally have good optical transmission performance. Thus, different from traditional rigid devices, scalable and deformable flexible electronic devices have a broader application prospect in biomedical and wearable electronic devices [8,9]. A high-performance flexible PD needs a new consideration in the selection of flexible substrate and photosensitive layer material. A useful method to obtain a flexible substrate is thinning down the traditional rigid substrate until it can withstand the bending strain. As the first generation of semiconductor material, silicon is very important in the field of semiconductor devices. Its bandgap energy of 1.12 eV can be used to detect the visible region. When the thickness of the silicon wafer is reduced to several tens of micrometers or less by chemical etching, the silicon wafer can exhibit flexibility. Compared with traditional silicon substrates, flexible ultrathin silicon reduces the weight of the device while achieving great flexibility.

To meet the requirement that the performance of flexible detectors cannot be significantly reduced after being repeatedly bent, the material used for the solar-blind UV detection should also possess sufficient ductility to meet a certain degree of bending requirements [10]. Low-dimensional wide-bandgap materials are an excellent choice as the photosensitive layer of flexible devices [11–13]. Carbon nanodots (CNDs), a new type of zero-dimension semiconductor nanocrystal that is approximately spherical, have been spotlighted as a potential material for fabricating the photosensitive layer since its optical band gap is tunable in the wavelength ranging from UV to deep UV [14]. Additionally, recent research has demonstrated that the introduction of CNDs boosts the photoelectric conversion efficiency of heterojunctions [15]. In our previous work [16], an electrochemical method to manufacture CNDs with excellent optical properties was proposed, which is simple and efficient. The CNDs prepared have significant absorption in the UV region, with a peak value in the wavelength of about 230 nm, which indicates that the absorption range of CNDs well covers the solar-blind UV wave band.

In this work, a dual-spectral CNDs/flexible silicon heterojunction PD is reported, in which the ultrathin silicon wafer is used as the flexible substrate, and CNDs are used as the photosensitive layer. When the CND film was terminated with the positive voltage, the device showed photocurrent under both visible and UV illumination. When the CND film was terminated with the reverse voltage, the device showed photocurrent only under UV illumination. Under 5 V bias, the responsivity of the device is 30.5 mA/W and 93.7 mA/W under the illumination of 254 nm and the visible illumination, respectively. And the detectivity is 1.6 × 1011 Jones and 2.14 × 1011 Jones, respectively. The photocurrent of the device can reach 91 % or more of the original under the 0–0.6 % bending strain. In the cyclic bending test, the device can withstand 500 bending cycles. After one month of storage in the natural environment, the detection performance of the device did not decrease, which indicates that the dual-spectral CNDs/flexible Si heterojunction PD has good stability.

The CNDs were obtained by the electrochemical method. Two graphite rods, with a length of 10 cm and a diameter of 0.6 cm, were prepared through ultrasonic cleaning by immersing them in ethanol and distilled water successively. Keep the two graphite rods at a fixed distance of 2 cm and insert them vertically into distilled water. With the rods as the electrodes and carbon source and distilled water as the electrolyte, the reaction was carried out at the DC voltage of 60 V. During the whole process, the solution was stirred with a magnetic stirrer. At first, the solution was colorless and transparent. As the reaction progressed, the solution changed its color from yellow to dark brown. After electrolysis, the solution was filtered with filter paper to remove the larger particles stripped and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 mins to remove the agglomerated CNDs. Triton-X 100 and PVA (mass ratio of 4 %) were used to mix with the CNDs solution: the CNDs solution and Triton-X 100/PVA were mixed uniformly at a volume ratio of 10:1, and ultrasonic mixing was performed for 20 mins.

The ultrathin monocrystalline silicon wafer was prepared by chemical etching. The specific steps are as follows. Firstly, the double-sided polished n-type silicon wafer with a thickness of 400 µm was cut into the size of 1.5 cm * 1 cm and then sonicated with acetone, ethanol, and deionized water for 10 mins, respectively. After the silicon wafers were dried, they were vertically fixed on the etching frame into the prepared 17.8 M potassium hydroxide (KOH) solution. The solution was heated with an etching temperature of 90 °C. Different thicknesses were obtained through the modulation of etching time. The etched silicon wafer was removed from the KOH solution and soaked in dilute hydrochloric acid separately for 2 h to neutralize the KOH on the surface of the silicon wafer. After soaking, rinse the silicon wafers with deionized water and dried them naturally.

Fig. 1(a) gives the preparation process of the PD. The ultrathin silicon wafer was fixed on the flexible polyethylene glycol terephthalate (PET) substrate, which can avoid damaging the ultrathin silicon wafer. Firstly, the CNDs were spin-coated on one side of the ultrathin silicon wafer in two steps, first at 500 rpm for 10 s and then at 2000 rpm for 30 s. And then the sample was annealed at 60 °C for 1 min. In this case, 15 layers of CNDs were coated, the purpose of which was to increase the thickness of the film and prevent direct contact between the top electrode and the silicon wafer. The metal aluminum was then thermally evaporated on the CNDs and silicon wafer as metal electrodes using the ZZS500–3/G model box vacuum coating machine. To increase the illumination area of the CNDs, the top electrode size should not be too large, estimated to be about 3 mm * 3 mm. The effective area of the CNDs and Si under illumination is about 0.91 cm2 and 1.50 cm2.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was taken using a transmission electron microscope (JEOL-02100, Japan). The current-voltage (I−V) curve and current-time (I-t) curve of the PDs were characterized using a probe station compatible with the Keithley 4200-SCS system semiconductor tester with a mercury lamp (OSRAM G11T5/ OF) of 254 nm wavelength as the solar-blind UV source and a white LED covering the wavelength from 400 nm to 610 nm as visible light source. All the operations were performed under ambient conditions and at room temperature.

Fig. 1(b)–(d) show the SEM diagram of the silicon wafer at the etching time of 2 h 20 mins, 2 h 30 mins, and 2 h 33 mins, with the thickness of 37.5 µm, 10.55 µm, and 8.75 µm, respectively. During the etching process, the thickness of the silicon wafer gradually decreased as the etching time increased, and in the late etching period, the etching rate was very fast. When the etching time increased to 2 h 35 mins, the wafer was almost completely dissolved in the etching solution. Fig. 1(e)–(g) give the flexibility of the etched silicon wafer with the thickness of 37.5 µm, 10.55 µm, and 8.75 µm, respectively. The smaller the thickness of the silicon wafer, the greater its maximum bending. However, the silicon wafer of 8.75 µm was very easy to be damaged due to its ultrathin thickness, and the success rate is too low in the process of device preparation. After comprehensive consideration, the silicon wafer of 10.55 µm is more in line with the requirements.

As shown in Fig. 2(a), the absorption spectra of the silicon wafers in the wavelength range of 400–1100 nm were measured. With the increase of the etching time, the optical absorption of the silicon wafer is gradually weakened in the visible region, indicating that too thin thickness will cause a decrease in the optical absorption capacity of the silicon wafer, which may affect the optical response performance of the device. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the CNDs prepared have significant absorption in the UV region, with a peak value in the wavelength of about 230 nm, which indicates that the absorption range of CNDs well covers the solar-blind UV wave band.

When the CNDs film is terminated with the positive pole and the silicon wafer is terminated with the negative pole, it is forward bias, otherwise, it is reverse bias. Fig. 2(c) shows the I-V curves of the device in the dark, 254 nm UV wavelength and visible wavelength, respectively. The current response of the device in the forward bias and the reverse bias was different. In the dark, the current was 1.01 μA when the applied voltage was 3 V, and the reverse current was 9.04 μA at − 3 V under 254 nm irradiation, the device generated obvious photocurrent at both forward and reverse voltages, while the device generated photocurrent only under forward bias voltage when exposed to visible light, and the current under reverse bias voltage was almost not changed.

The photocurrent responsivity (R) and normalized detectivity (D*) are two significant parameters of PDs. R represents the ratio of the photocurrent to the input optical signal power, and can be calculated as the following formula:
(1)
R =
I
ph
A
E
E
#
where I ph is the photocurrent under the UV illumination of 254 nm, A is the illumination area of the samples, and E e is the incident light irradiance.

D* describes the ability to detect weak optical signals per unit area (1 cm2) and per unit bandwidth (1 Hz). Assuming that the noise is mainly scattered noise, the formula can be defined as:
(2)
D
*
=
R
A
2 e
I
dark
where R is the responsivity above, A is the illumination area of the samples, e is the electron charge, and I dark is the dark current.

The I-V curves of the device under 254 nm light irradiation with different optical power are shown in Fig. 2(d). The power densities of the light source range from 0.49 to 1.47 mW/cm2. As the light power increased, the photocurrent of the device also increased. The curve is fitted well by the power law as the formula below:
(3)
I = A
P
α
#
where I is the photocurrent, P is the incident light power, A is the constant, and α is the power value after the curve fitting. The ideal value of α is 1, exhibiting that the photocurrent is linear to the optical power. When the value deviates from 1, it indicates that there is complex electron-hole recombination in the PDs due to the recombination center and traps [17,18]. In our case, Fig. 2(e) gives the experimental and fitting curves of the corresponding photocurrent as a function of light intensities at − 5 V, and the fitted value α was estimated to be 0.74, which is limited by complex electron-hole recombination process caused by recombination centers and traps. The curve of responsivity with incident optical power is shown in Fig. 2(f). The R of PD first increased slightly with the increase of irradiation power, and then decreased with the increase of optical power, with a maximum R of 30.5 mA/W.

Similarly, Fig. 2(g) shows the I-V curves in the visible region at different optical powers. The photocurrent under visible wavelength also increased with the increase of light power, but the increase under reverse bias was not obvious, which also indicates that the phenomenon of almost no response of the device, when illuminated with visible light under negative bias, was not caused by low light power, but by the intrinsic mechanism of the device. Fig. 2(h) exhibits the current-power curve of the device at 5 V, fitted with α value of 0.77. The curve of R with incident irradiation power is shown in Fig. 2(i). The R also rose slightly with the increase of light power, and then decreased with the increase of light power, with a maximum R of 93.7 mA/W. This phenomenon can be explained as that when the incident optical power is small, as the optical power increases, more incident photons will be excited, and the device can produce more photogenerated carriers. When the power is further increased, the number of photogenerated carriers is increasing, and the accumulated carriers will decrease the strength of the built-in electric field, producing a shielding effect. The decrease of the built-in electric field can result in a slower separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and increase the possibility of carrier recombination, leading to a reduction in the responsivity.

Furthermore, the maximum D* at irradiation with 254 nm and visible light was 1.61 × 1011 Jones and 2.14 × 1011 Jones at 5 V bias, respectively. Due to the lack of background noise from the surrounding environment, detectors with a D* higher than 108 Jones can usually be seen as good solar-blind UV photodetection. Therefore, it can be said that this CNDs/ultrathin-silicon heterojunction PD has a reliable detection performance in the solar-blind UV region.

To study the stability of the device, the I-V characteristic of the CNDs/ultrathin n-Si heterojunction PD under illumination is depicted in Fig. 2(j), which is consistent with the trend in Fig. 2(d). The device can stably respond to chopped 254 nm radiation in the bias range of − 5 to 5 V. A linear variation model was used to fit the discrete data points to estimate the rise time and decay time by calculating the time for the current to rise from 10 % to 90 % of the peak value and the current to drop from 90 % to 10 % of the peak value. As shown in Fig. 2(k), the rise time (τr) and decay time (τd) of the PD are estimated to be 0.604 s and 0.224 s respectively. The response time is limited by the oxygen-assisted mechanism in which the photogenerated electrons are captured by oxygen atoms, which are absorbed by surface defects. The oxygen-assisted recombination of the electron/hole pair leads to the rise of the carrier lifetime, thus decreasing the response time [19,20].

To calculate the strain applied in CNDs/flexible Silicon layers, a variable ε was introduced as [21,22]:
(4)
ε =
d
f
+
d
s
2 R
#
where d f is the film thickness, d s is the substrate thickness, and R is the bending radius. The film thickness is negligible, which is usually at the nanoscale, much smaller than the substrate thickness.

The PDs were prepared with flexible silicon wafers, which were approximately 10.55 µm in thickness, and tested for their detection performance at ε = 0 %, 0.2 %, 0.3 %, and 0.6 %. Fig. 3(a) shows that the device has excellent switching response characteristics under all four bending strains. The dark current was almost unchanged. Under the irradiance of 254 nm wavelength, the photocurrent was 97 %, 98 %, and 91 % of the original at ε = 0.2 %, 0.3 %, and 0.6 %, respectively, as presented in Fig. 3(b). Fig. 3(c) shows that the response time of the device changed little when the strain increased, which indicates the stability of the device. Fig. 3(d) shows the curve of R and D* of the device with strain at a bias of 3 V. As the strain increased, both the R and the D* decreased slightly. The maximum R of the device was 21.17 mA/W, and the minimum was 18.7 mA/W. The maximum D* was 6.17 × 1010 Jones, and the minimum was 5.45 × 1010 Jones.

The photo response of flexible PD may decay irreversibly [23] under cyclic stress, so the stability of the CNDs/ultrathin-silicon heterojunction solar-blind UV PD was tested after multiple cyclic stresses. Fig. 3(e) gives the I-t curve of PD after different cycles of bending at ε = 0.2 %. As can be seen, when the device was bent 10 times, the photocurrent dropped significantly to about 92 % of origin, which is greater than the change of dark current. When the bend cycles rose to 50, the dark current decreased slightly, and the photocurrent dropped to about 84 %. After the bend cycles further increased from 100 times to 500 times, the performance of the device was relatively stable and the photocurrent can remain at the original 84 %. As can be seen from Fig. 3(f), the response time of the device did not change much within the 500-bend cycles.

The long-term effectiveness of the device was tested under 254 nm, 0.3 mW/cm2, at 3 V after placing it for a month in the natural environment and at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 4, the PD had a stable switching response for around a month, without any significant change in photocurrent, dark current, and optical response speed. This also further demonstrates that PD has excellent long-term effectiveness. Combined with the performance of the device after many bend cycles, the flexible CNDs/silicon heterojunction PD has eminent stability.

To get a deeper insight into the working mechanism of the PD under external bias and illumination, the band structure of the PD was further investigated. The CNDs and silicon wafers are n-type doped. The bandgap energy of the CNDs is 4.6 eV as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(b) and the bandgap energy of the silicon wafer is 1.12 eV. Fig. 5(a) shows that under the 254 nm illumination, both CNDs and silicon can absorb photons and produce photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which are rapidly separated under the built-in electric field. Under the forward bias, due to the reduction of the barrier height, photoelectrons can cross the barrier from the silicon to the CNDs, and together with the photoelectrons generated in the CNDs, they can flow to the positive electrode and enter the external circuit. Photogenerated holes from the CNDs can also cross the barrier and flow from the silicon to the negative electrode together with the photogenerated holes from the silicon region. Electrons and holes can be recycled in the circuit, manifested as the device generating a photocurrent. When the PD is irradiated with visible light, there are no photo-generated electron-hole pairs in the CNDs, because the photon energy of visible light is less than the bandgap energy of the CNDs. Meanwhile, only the silicon produces the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, as shown in Fig. 5(b). Under the external electric field, photogenerated electrons cross the barrier from the silicon through the CNDs to the positive electrode, photogenerated holes flow to the negative electrode into the external circuit, and carriers are circulated to form the photocurrent. Hence, only the photogenerated carriers generated by silicon contribute to the photocurrent during visible light irradiation. So at a positive voltage of 3 V, the device exhibited a dual-band response from solar-blind UV to visible wavelength with a peak at 254 nm, as shown in Fig. 5(e).

Similarly, the carrier flow of the device exposed to the illumination at reverse bias is shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d). Under the 254 nm illumination, both the CNDs and silicon regions produce photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Under the external electric field, the photogenerated carriers circulate in the circuit, while the holes in the silicon cannot circulate due to the barrier. Therefore, the photocurrent is smaller than that under the positive bias. When the PD is exposed to visible light, the silicon region produces photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and the holes cannot flow to the negative electrode due to the barrier. Thus, photogenerated carriers cannot form cycles in the circuit. Compared to under the forward bias, the device exhibited almost no photoresponsivity to visible light under reserve bias, as shown in Fig. 5(e). Meanwhile, there was still a certain responsivity to solar-blind UV radiation. To sum up, this work achieves voltage-adjustable dual-band solar-blind UV–visible photodetection.

Furthermore, Table 1 gives a comparison of performance between our device and some of the previously reported similar structures of solar-blind UV PDs. What should be emphasized is that the photoresponsivity of our device is greater than any other similar structures of solar-blind UV PD in Table 1 (published in recent years) [22–26]. For those presenting response time close to this work, they either suffer from limited wavelength selectivity or can’t show high responsivity with low bias. According to the investigation above, it can be concluded that our device exhibits performance superiority to peer work by maintaining decent photoresponsivity, Detectivity, and response time without the requirement of applying a high biased voltage.

In summary, a dual-spectral CNDs/flexible silicon heterojunction photodetector was demonstrated in this work. The device responded in both the visible and UV bands under forward bias, and only responded in the UV under reverse bias, optimizing performance trade-off with superior stability. Thus, our work proves that the combination of CNDs and ultrathin silicon can improve the ability of flexible photodetectors in complex environments, which paves a new way to endow the next-generation solar-blind ultraviolet photodetectors.

Taihao Chen (First Author): Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yong Fang (Co-First Author): Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Mengru Zhu: Visualization, Investigation. Wei Lei: Resources, Supervision. Zhuoya Zhu: Validation. Helong Jiang: Validation. Zhiwei Zhao (Corresponding Author): Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Scientific Instrument Developing Project of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (YJKYYQ20190050) and the Carbon Peak/Neutralization Technology Innovation Project of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20220043).

Synth. Met.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Abdel-Khalek H., Shalaan E., Abd-El Salam M., et al. Effect of illumination intensity on the characteristics of Cu(acac)2/n-Si photodiode. Synthetic Metals, 2018, 245:233–236.DOI:10.1016/j.synthmet.2018.09.009.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhiwei Zhao received the Ph.D. degree in Microelectronics from Nanyang Technological University, Singapore, in 2004. She has published more than 50 peer-reviewed journal articles as the first or corresponding author. She is currently a Full Professor at the School of Electronic Science and Engineering, Southeast University. Her research interests include the development of micro/nano functional materials and their applications in new energy, sensors, electronic devices, Power management systems, and Measurement and collection of weak voltage signals.
